coredata.dc:title: Microwave assisted green synthesis of carbon dots from sweet flag (Acorus calamus) for fluorescent sensing of 4-nitrophenol
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Venugopalan, P.
Vidya, N.
coredata.dc:description: In this piece of work, a simple and cost-effective fluorescence sensor for the detection of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) was developed from natural resource, sweet flag (Acorus calamus) through greener route by the use of microwave energy. 4-NP is identified as a potential water pollutant by EPA which can exert several hazardous effect in humans and other aquatic organisms and hence lower level detection of 4-NP is highly demanding. The as obtained carbon dots with excitation dependent fluorescence behaviour, designated as SFCDs, produced a noticeable quenching in fluorescence on interacting with 4-NP. Therefore, a simple and time-saving fluorescence sensing for the detection of 4-NP was developed. The observed turn-off in fluorescence of SFCDs with 4-NP was investigated through different experiments and finally accredited to a combination of both static quenching and inner filter effect (IFE). The proposed probe selectively detect the 4-NP with LOD of 0.207 µM and linear range of 0–14.28 µM, the detection limit was found to be lower than allowed concentration of this pollutant in drinking water. Furthermore, the same method has been practically applied for detecting 4-NP in natural water resources with good level of recovery percentages. As a whole, the developed method gives value addition to the sweet flag for the first time by its direct materialization through a completely greener route without using any hazardous chemicals. The good level of precision and accuracy with detection limits in micromolar range are promising to use the system as a fluoroprobe for detecting 4-NP, a primary pollutant found in natural water resources.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescent sensor
4-Nitrophenol
Static quenching
Inner filter effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Microwave assisted synthesis of carbon dots
Synthesis and characterization of carbon dots
Fluorescent assay of 4-NP in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge Sree Neelakanta Government Sanskrit College Pattambi, University of Calicut and FIST program of DST for providing the facilities. SAIF, Cochin University of Science and Technology, SAIF, Mahatma Gandhi University, Kottayam and PSG IAS, Coimbatore are also acknowledged for experimental support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a, b, c) TEM images of SFCDs with different scale (d) Histogram from (a) n = 15.
(a) XRD pattern (b) Raman spectrum (c) FTIR spectrum of SFCDs.

(a)Normalized UV–vis absorption spectrum of SFCDs (b)Fluorescence emission spectrum of SFCDs (c)Excitation dependent emission spectra of SFCDs from 325 to 435 nm (d)Excitation and emission spectra of SFCDs (inset: SFCDs at normal light(left) and SFCDs at UV light(right)).

(a)Relative fluorescence intensity of SFCDs with different metal ions(1 to 12 representing Blank, K+, Zn2+, Na+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Fe3+and 4-NP) (b)Relative fluorescence intensity with different phenolic compounds (1 to 12 representing phenol, resorcinol, 4-chlorophenol, 2-methylphenol, 3-methylphenol, 4-methylphenol, 3-nitrophenol, 2-nitrophenol, 4-NP, 4-NP with 2-nitrophenol,4-NP with Fe3+ and 4-NP along with Fe3+ and 2-nitrophenol).(c)Fluorescence emission spectra with increasing concentration of 4-NP (inset shows the photographs of SFCDs (left) and SFCDs with 4-NP(right) under normal and UV light) (d) Linear relationships between relative fluorescence with concentration of 4-NP.

(a)Fluorescence life time decay curve of SFCDs with and without 4-NP (b) Normalized UV–vis absorption spectra of SFCDs, 4-NP(4-NP), SFCDs@4-NP (SFCDs@4-NP) and SFCDs + 4-NP (c)Spectral overlap of excitation and emission spectra of SFCDs with absorbance spectrum of 4-NP.
Comparison of different sensors for 4-NP detection.

row.entry: Boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (B,N-CDs)
Carbon dots derived from sweet flag

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detection of 4-NP in different water samples.

head.ce:title: Microwave assisted green synthesis of carbon dots from sweet flag (Acorus calamus) for fluorescent sensing of 4-nitrophenol

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Sree Neelakanta Government Sanskrit College (Affiliated to University of Calicut), Pattambi 679306, Kerala, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Sree Neelakanta Government Sanskrit College (Affiliated to University of Calicut)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Sree Neelakanta Government Sanskrit College (Affiliated to University of Calicut), Pattambi 679306, Kerala, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this piece of work, a simple and cost-effective fluorescence sensor for the detection of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) was developed from natural resource, sweet flag (Acorus calamus) through greener route by the use of microwave energy. 4-NP is identified as a potential water pollutant by EPA which can exert several hazardous effect in humans and other aquatic organisms and hence lower level detection of 4-NP is highly demanding. The as obtained carbon dots with excitation dependent fluorescence behaviour, designated as SFCDs, produced a noticeable quenching in fluorescence on interacting with 4-NP. Therefore, a simple and time-saving fluorescence sensing for the detection of 4-NP was developed. The observed turn-off in fluorescence of SFCDs with 4-NP was investigated through different experiments and finally accredited to a combination of both static quenching and inner filter effect (IFE). The proposed probe selectively detect the 4-NP with LOD of 0.207 µM and linear range of 0–14.28 µM, the detection limit was found to be lower than allowed concentration of this pollutant in drinking water. Furthermore, the same method has been practically applied for detecting 4-NP in natural water resources with good level of recovery percentages. As a whole, the developed method gives value addition to the sweet flag for the first time by its direct materialization through a completely greener route without using any hazardous chemicals. The good level of precision and accuracy with detection limits in micromolar range are promising to use the system as a fluoroprobe for detecting 4-NP, a primary pollutant found in natural water resources.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Sweet flag (Acorus calamus) derived carbon dots

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: 4-Nitrophenol (4-NP) is an important and relevant aromatic compound, has been well recognized to play major role in the manufacture of several industrial products, like agrochemical products, explosives, textiles, leather, and pharmaceuticals, etc [1,2]. These products are very much essential for the well-being of the whole society. However, the acute toxicity, lesser bio-degradability, high stability and intense water solubility of this compound make it as a dangerous chemical contaminant, and U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) enlisted it as priority pollutant and maximum accepted level of this contaminant in drinking water has been restricted to be below 0.43 µM [3]. In addition to use for the preparation of many useful pharma chemicals including paracetamol, it is also get released by widely used organophosphate pesticides like parathion, fenitrothion, etc. and thus the reach of this pollutant to water bodies is pretty much easier. Even at small level concentration,4-NP can exerts its adverse effect; this pollutant has been found to impart hazardous effects on the health of all beings. The accumulation of 4-NP in human body results severe health issues including nervous and ocular system malfunctioning, cyto-embryonic and mutagenic disorders [3]. Hence, it is highly demandable to design and develop simple, rapid, reliable and efficient method for the detection of 4-NP in water samples.

Till date, most widely used analytical methods for the detection of 4-NP in water samples are electrochemical methods [4], capillary electrophoresis [5], spectrophotometric methods [6], high-performance liquid chromatography [7] and fluorescence methods [8]. Amongst the listed methods, fluorescence based detection carries more advantages such as operational simplicity, real-time detection, less sample requirement and higher reproducibility [2]. Fluorescence based sensing probes receives fascinating level of acceptance in recent years due to the aforementioned features [9–12].

Among the various fluorescent probes, fluorescent carbon nano-materials based systems especially carbon dots withhold considerable level of reception due to its several salient features including, good level of water solubility, biocompatibility, lesser cytotoxicity, commendable chemical stability, reproducibility, efficient and controllable optical properties, and excellent eco-friendlily nature. Carbon dots have been accepted as the most greener and efficient nano candidate from the carbon nano section with particle size under 10 nm. The excellent, bright and tunable fluorescence property of this super material makes its acceptance more feasible [13–15]. Owing to the above mentioned properties carbon dots have been find application in diverse fields like electrochemical sensing and electro catalysis [16,17], photo catalysis [18], nano medicine and biomedical [19–21], fluorescence, bio sensing and imaging [22–24].

In recent times, biogenic carbon dots gather much more attention due its merits over the hazardous chemical based carbon dots. Non-toxic nature, bio-availability and cost effectiveness are the major advantages of the bio precursor based carbon dots, most importantly the greener character of them make large contribution towards this acceptance [25,26]. The present work gives value addition to a pharmacologically important natural precursor, sweet flag (Acorus calamus) by converting it in to fluorescent carbon dots. This materialization was successfully completed with the aid of microwave irradiation without using any hazardous chemicals or sophisticated experimental setups. Homogeneous and direct heating, time saving reaction procedures and eco-friendly nature of the microwave synthetic method makes it dominant over the other synthetic methods under use [27,28]. Sweet flag rhizomes widely accepted in Ayurvedic medicinal system and its pharmacological importance are well studied [29], and reducing potential of its constituents were used for the synthesis of silver nanoparticles [30]. However, to the best of our knowledge, its direct materialization is not reported till date.

Herein, carbon dots with bright fluorescence were prepared from an important bio resource by strictly following green protocols in every aspect. The as prepared system showed selective interaction with 4-NP and this interaction was explored to develop a fluorescence sensor for the above water pollutant. After a series of studies, the mechanism of interaction was attributed to combination of static quenching and IFE. The practicability of the probe was tested by employing the sensor towards real water samples. The observed detection limit in micro scale range, reproducibility and good statistical parameters are promising to use the system as a potential nanoprobe for the detection of 4-NP in natural water bodies.

The dried rhizomes of sweet flag were obtained from the local market in Kerala, India. All the chemicals were used as such without further purification. Metal salts (Potassium chloride, zinc acetate, sodium chloride, calcium chloride, lead nitrate, mercuric chloride, cobalt(II) nitrate, copper(II) sulfate, cadmium nitrate and ferric chloride) and phenolic compounds were procured from Merck Millipore, India. Distilled water was used for all purposes throughout the work. Real water samples used in this study were collected locally.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were used to analyze the particle size morphology and size distribution of the prepared system (SFCDs). Images were obtained through JEOL JEM 2100 transmission electron microscope of 0.23 nm point resolution and 0.14 nm lattice resolution using LaB6 electron gun with 200 kV accelerating voltage. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the SFCDs was recorded using PANalytical X’pert3 Powder X-ray diffractometer. Extent of disorder associated with SFCDs examined through Raman spectrum and was obtained by confocal Raman Microscope (WiTec alpha 300, Germany). Surface functionalities of SFCDs was explored using Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FTIR) of the system and the same was obtained from PerkinElmer Fourier transform infrared spectrum Instrument (500 to 4000 cm−1), with an average scan rate of 64 scans. Optical properties of SFCDs were examined by the use of UV–vis spectrophotometer and fluorescent spectrophotometer. PerkinElmer UV–vis spectrophotometer of scan range 200–800 nm and PerkinElmer FL-6500 fluorescence spectrometer were used for the absorbance and fluorescence studies, respectively. All the fluorescence and absorbance measurements were done at 25°C. Fluorescence quantum yield was determined through standard method using quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 as reference standard, using equation (1)
QSFCDs = QS [MSFCDs/ MS](Ƞ2 SFCDs/Ƞ2 S) (1)

where, QSFCDs and QS indicates quantum yield of SFCDs and standard reference, respectively. MSFCDs and MS represent the slope obtained from the linear curve from the graph drawn by integrated fluorescence intensity and UV–vis absorbance of SFCDs and reference standard, respectively and Ƞ stand for the refractive index of the solvents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microwave assisted synthesis of carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: An eco-friendly method was opted for the synthesis of carbon dots from sweet flag. Briefly, about 10 g of dried sweet flag were magnetically stirred with 30 mL of hot distilled water for 30 min. The resultant solution filtered and irradiated with microwaves in a domestic microwave oven for 25 min at 500 W with frequent cooling in every 5 min. The brownish dry residue obtained was then stirred magnetically with distilled water and centrifuged for 15 min to remove large particles. Besides, the resultant solution was dialyzed with dialysis membrane of 3 KDa MWCO against distilled water for 48 h. Then the as obtained yellowish brown solution was kept at 4 °C for further use.

The detection of 4-NP was carried out by measuring the fluorescence intensity of the SFCDs in presence of different concentration of 4-NP. In a typical assay, 2 mL of SFCDs solution was taken in quartz cuvette and fluorescence spectrum of the solution measured. Afterwards different concentrations of 4-NP (0–14.28 µM) solution were introduced to the system by the use of micropipette and fluorescence spectra of the mixture were monitored after 1 min of incubation time. The intensity variation up on addition of 4-NP was carefully noticed and recorded. The same procedure was followed for the selectivity studies by replacing 4-NP with other substances like metal ions (K+, Zn2+, Na+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Hg2+, Cu2+ and Fe3+) and phenolic compounds (phenol, resorcinol, 4-chlorophenol, 2-methylphenol, 3-methylphenol, 4-methylphenol, 3-nitrophenol and 2-nitrophenol).

The detection performance of the nanoprobe was practically tested by implementing it for the detection of 4-NP in real water samples collected locally (River water and tap water). River water sampleswere collected from the nearby Nila River and the tap water samples collected from the laboratory tap. The studies were carried out using standard addition method. Firstly, the samples were filtered to remove large particles and then centrifuged for 20 min. Then these samples were spiked with different concentration of standard 4-NP solution and this mixture was added to the probe solution and after incubation of 1 min the fluorescence spectrum was recorded using the same instrument and under the same experimental conditions used for all other fluorescence studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: SFCDs were derived from the dried rhizomes of sweet flag by microwave irradiation method. This microwave heating instigates the formation carbon nano particles from the extract; consists with large number of different organic molecules including eugenol acetate, cineole, butyric acid, polyphenolic acids and ester of butyric acids, several amino acids and carbohydrates [29,31]. The constituents of the precursor plays vital role in the formation of carbon dots from the extract. The proposed mechanism follows the bottom-up scheme, which involves the formation of nano dimensional particles from smaller molecules when subjected to pyrolysis. The mechanism majorly consists of different steps including condensation, polymerisation, carbonization and nuclear burst resulting into nano products. In brief, during the microwave irradiation, the carbohydrates present in the extract undergo different chemical reactions such as hydrolysis, dehydration and decomposition. These reactions are facilitated by the polyphenolic acids present in the extract. In general, the furfural derivatives were formed by the above reactions undergoes cascade of reaction including self-condensation and reaction with other reactive molecules in the system leads to the formation of different products and these may get polymerized up on polymerization reaction. The condensation and aromatization of these polymerised products leads to the carbon cluster generation and finally the nuclear burst of the cluster gives off the fluorescent carbon dots. All of the reactions proceeds by the aid of microwave energy within a short duration of time [27,32].

FTIR spectrum of the SFCDs (Fig. 2c) was used to identify the functionalities associated with surface of SFCDs. A broad peak centered at 3292 cm−1 corresponds to stretching vibrations of OH and NH bonds. While small peaks at 2932 and 2831 cm-1 were attributed to CH stretching vibrations [33,36]. A strong band at 1082 cm−1 associated with the CO stretching vibrations from alcoholic as well as carboxylic groups in the surface of SFCDs. Comparatively strong band at 1578 cm−1 was assumed to be contributed by the combined effect of CC stretching, NH bending vibrations and COO– asymmetric stretching vibrations. The symmetric stretching vibration of COO– gives rise to the small band at 1405 cm−1 [37]. Whereas peak at 1452 cm−1 assigned CN stretching vibrations [38]. Altogether the FTIR data reveals that the surface of SFCDs contains various hydrophilic functionalities including carboxylic, amino and hydroxyl groups. These groups efficiently contribute to the prominent water solubility and stability of SFCDs. This solubility and stability of SFCDs in aqueous medium terribly expands its applicability as sensor in water samples. The photo-stability of the system was tested by placing the solution under UV light of 365 nm for one hour shows no significant fluorescence reduction implies that the system exhibits good level of photo-resistance.

The intriguing optical characteristics of the SFCDs were well explored by the UV–vis absorption spectra and photoluminescence spectra. As given in Fig. 3 a, there exits two absorption peaks around 227 and 304 nm, the former one assigned to the π - π* transition from the aromatic cores of SFCDs and later one ascribed to the electronic transitions originating from the non-bonding electrons of the functionalities present in the system. The present system shows yellowish brown color under normal light but having greenish blue fluorescence under UV light of 365 nm. The photograph of the same is given in the inset of Fig. 3b along with excitation and emission spectra. SFCDs exhibits excitation dependent emission behavior, as the excitation wavelength alters emission wavelength as well as emission intensity also changed and this behavior of the system may attribute to the polydispesity and surface emissive traps [33]. The fluorescence spectra with excitation wavelength ranging from 325 to 435 nm with interval of 10 nm are given in Fig. 3c. From the excitation dependence studies, it reveals that SFCDs possessing highest emission intensity when excited at 365 nm and it was centered at 441 nm. So these values were selected as the excitation and emission wavelength of the present probe for further studies. The fluorescence quantum yield of the SFCDs was determined to be about 5.4 % by standard method with quinine sulfate as reference standard.

To investigate the possibility of the system as fluorescent sensing probe on account to its prominent fluorescence behavior, various fluorescent studies were performed. Primarily, fluorescence responses of the probe with different metal ions (K+, Zn2+, Na+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Hg2+, Cu2+ and Fe3+) were recorded along with 4-NP, considering the fact that metal ions were commonly exerts quenching or enhancing effects with carbon dots. The results show that 4-NP exhibits significant fluorescence reduction (Fig. 4 a). In addition, taking into account the probable interaction of phenols with surface functionalities of SFCDs, fluorescence responses of probe with different phenolic compounds were also monitored. As shown in Fig. 4b, among the isomeric mono nitrophenols, 3-nitrophenol shows negligible effect on fluorescence. 4-NP displays highest fluorescence quenching efficiency with 32 % reduction in fluorescence followed by 2-nitrophenol, having only 12 % fluorescence reduction. This confirms the selectivity of the probe towards 4-NP. The major reason for this selectivity of 4-NP than the other two mononitrophenols is the comparatively stronger interaction of the SFCDs with the 4-NP. The strong acidic nature of the species over the other two plays vital role in the stronger interaction with the surface functionalities of the SFCDs. Stronger electron withdrawing effect of the nitro group in the 4-NP make it more acidic. The electron rich amino groups present in the SFCDs surface interact with the 4-NP and leads to fluorescence quenching [39]. To further ensure the selectivity of the probe, the interference studies were conducted by Fe3+ and 2-nitrophenol along with 4-NP, since these two species are showing some amount of quenching efficiency on comparing others, and the result displayed in Fig. 4b shows that these foreign species does not exerts any significant effect in the sensing of 4-NP. The fluorescence reduction of SFCDs by 4-NP is about three fold higher than the case of 2-nitrophenol and may be due to this it does not exerts much effect in the sensing performance of SFCDs towards 4-NP, similar in the case of Fe3+ where the reduction in fluorescence of SFCDs is about 20 % as compared to 4-NP. Moreover, the presence of 2-nitrophenol in water resources was comparatively lower than that of 4-NP and this may be due to its lower solubility in water.

To explore the sensitivity of 4-NP towards the system, fluorescence studies with different concentration of 4-NP were performed. The fluorescence spectrum of the bare SFCDs was recorded initially and then different concentration of 4-NP was introduced to the system. The corresponding fluorescence spectra were monitored after incubation time of 1 min, after optimization by trial and error method. Fig. 4c clearly reveals the gradual decline of fluorescence intensity of the system by the addition quenching species. As the concentration of quencher increases the intensity fluorescence decreases. The quenching behavior follows linear relationships with concentration of the quencher from 0 to 14.28 µM. The linear plot of relative fluorescence intensity with concentration of 4-NP was displayed in Fig. 4d. The LOD was found to be 0.207 µM (28.79 ng/mL) and it was calculated by the standard equation 3σ/κ, where σ is the standard deviation and κ is the slope of linear plot obtained from the linear equation F0 /F = 0.0629 [4-NP] + 0.9889. The LOD value and other statistical parameters obtained for this system was comparable with the recent reports in literature and some of them are enlisted in Table 1 . Moreover, the detection limit is lesser than the accepted level (60 ng/mL) of 4-NP in drinking water by US EPA [33], indicates that the probe developed can be implemented to the real water analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent assay of 4-NP in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the practicability of this sensing method, SFCDs were used for the detection of 4-NP in river and tap water samples by standard addition method. The pre-treated water samples were spiked with standard 4-NP solution and then mixed with SFCDs probe. The mixtures were incubated for 1 min. Afterwards, fluorescence spectrum of the mixture was recorded at excitation of 365 nm. Subsequently, different concentration of 4-NP was added, fluorescence spectra were monitored and recovery and error percentages were calculated. The results obtained are tabulated in Table 2 . As given, the recovery percentage ranges from 94.80 to 97.20 %.

The sensing mechanism behind the selective sensing of 4-NP by the fabricated nanoprobe was thoroughly investigated. In general fluorescence quenching mechanism can be of four main types, namely static quenching mechanism, dynamic quenching mechanism, IFE and FRET (Förster resonance energy transfer) [45]. The fluorescence decay studies of SFCDs without and with the quenching species helps to reveal the possible mechanism. The results of the same is shown in Fig. 5 a, The average lifetime of SFCDs was determined to be 6.891 ns and that of SFCDs@4-NP was 6.783 ns, indicates that there is no significant change in the life time of SFCDs by the addition of 4-NP. This result excludes the chance of dynamic and FRET quenching mechanism from the list [45,46]. Then the quenching may follow static quenching mechanism or IFE mechanism. To reveal this the possibility of complex formation between 4-NP and amino group in the surface of SFCDs was investigated. The electron deficient 4-NP due to strong electron withdrawing effect of nitro group at the para position interacts effectively with the electron rich amino group in the surface of SFCDs results in the formation of Meisenheimer complex. This complex formation may leads to the fluorescence quenching through electron transfer induced fluorescence quenching [33]. To furthermore confirming the complex formation, UV–vis spectra of 4-NP, SFCDs and SFCDs with 4-NP (SFCDs@4-NP) were recorded. The spectra are shown in Fig. 5b. As seen in Fig. 5b, the absorbance spectrum of SFCDs@4-NP differs from that of SFCDs and 4-NP. Besides, the experimental spectrum of 4-NP with SFCDs was different from the theoretical spectrum, indicates the presence of effective interaction between the 4-NP and SFCDs. Moreover, there is an extra peak centered at 459 nm in absorbance spectrum of SFCDs@4-NP and it is not present either in the spectrum of SFCDs, 4-NP, or theoretical spectrum of SFCDs + 4-NP. Therefore, the new peak in the absorbance spectrum of SFCDs@4-NP may be accredited to the complex formed between the quencher and surface functional groups of SFCDs. Additionally, the quenching effect was also determined by using the Stern-Volmer equation (equation (2)), from the linear plot of relative fluorescence response with concentration of quenching species, by linear regression with fixed intercept at 1.

Where, Ksv is the Stern-Volmer constant, Kq is the quenching rate constant and τ0 is the average life time. The value of Kq for this probe was determined to be 8.96 × 1012 M−1s−1 greater than the highest value of dynamic quenching effect is about 1.0 × 1010 M−1s−1.This finding leads to the conclusion that the mechanism of 4-NP sensing involved a complex formation between the quencher and fluorescent system. This type of mechanism generally belongs to static quenching mechanism. To test the role of IFE in the quenching mechanism, the UV–vis spectrum of 4-NP was compared with the excitation and emission spectrum of SFCDs. The UV–vis spectrum of 4-NP consists of two major peaks one at 315 nm accredited to 4-NP and another one centered at 400 nm originates from its ions, being acidic 4-NP easily forms corresponding phenolate ions in water medium [47]. Surprisingly it was found that there is slight overlap between the absorbance peak at 400 nm with both excitation and emission spectrum of SFCDs, so IFE can also contributes to the fluorescence quenching of SFCDs by 4-NP. Therefore, the underlying mechanism of the fluorescence quenching of present system by 4-NP is a combination of static quenching mechanism and slight IFE.

In summary, a pharmacologically relevant bio-precursor was successfully converted to highly fluorescent carbon dots through facial, cost-effective and simple pathway by truly following green strategies and protocols. Synthesis through microwave irradiation without using any hazardous chemicals or complicated instruments adds more environmental benign color to the system. The selective interaction of the nano probe with 4-NP with quenching of native fluorescence of the system was utilized to design and fabricate fluorescent sensing tool for the detection of 4-NP. The developed sensor exhibits micromolar level of detection limit and keeps linearity relationships with the concentration of quencher. The quenching mechanism underlying this sensing was thoroughly investigated and attributed to the combination effect of static and IFE mechanism. Moreover the probe was applied to real water analysis to test the potentiality of the tool. The recovery percentage and other parameters give highly acceptable levels, implies that the system can be used as a promising tool for the sensing of 4-NP in natural water systems. Importantly, the proposed method not only gives value addition to the precursor but also give rise to a sensing platform for the detection of aquatic pollutant in natural water resources.
P. Venugopalan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology. N. Vidya: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge Sree Neelakanta Government Sanskrit College Pattambi, University of Calicut and FIST program of DST for providing the facilities. SAIF, Cochin University of Science and Technology, SAIF, Mahatma Gandhi University, Kottayam and PSG IAS, Coimbatore are also acknowledged for experimental support.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Catalytic hydrogenation of p-nitrophenol using a metal-free catalyst of porous crimped graphitic carbon nitride
Appl. Surf. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Huang, Y. Fu, Q. Peng, C. Yu, J. Zhu, A. Yu, X. Wang, Catalytic hydrogenation of p-nitrophenol using a metal-free catalyst of porous crimped graphitic carbon nitride, Applied Surface Science. 480 (2019) 888–895. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2019.03.035.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitrogen, silicon co-doped carbon dots as the fluorescence nanoprobe for trace p-nitrophenol detection based on inner filter effect
Spectrochim Acta, Part A.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Wang, Y. Liu, Q. Wang, T. Bu, X. Sun, P. Jia, L. Wang, Nitrogen, silicon co-doped carbon dots as the fluorescence nanoprobe for trace p-nitrophenol detection based on inner filter effect, Spectrochim. Acta, Part A. 244 (2021) 118876. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2020.118876.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: 4-nitrophenol optical sensing with N doped oxidized carbon dots
J. Hazard. Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N.K.R. Bogireddy, R. Cruz Silva, M.A. Valenzuela, V. Agarwal, 4-nitrophenol optical sensing with N doped oxidized carbon dots, J. Hazard. Mater. 386 (2020) 121643. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jhazmat.2019.121643.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical detection of 4-nitrophenol based on a glassy carbon electrode modified with a reduced graphene oxide/Au nanoparticle composite
Anal. Methods.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Tang, R. Huang, C. Liu, S. Yang, Z. Lu, S. Luo, Electrochemical detection of 4-nitrophenol based on a glassy carbon electrode modified with a reduced graphene oxide/Au nanoparticle composite, Anal. Methods. 5 (2013) 5508–5514. https://doi.org/10.1039/C3AY40742J.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Separation and detection of nitrophenols at capillary electrophoresis microchips with amperometric detection
Electroanalysis.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Fischer, J. Barek, J. Wang, Separation and Detection of Nitrophenols at Capillary Electrophoresis Microchips with Amperometric Detection, Electroanalysis. 18 (2006) 195–199. https://doi.org/10.1002/elan.200503393.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Kinetic spectrophotometric method for 4-nitrophenol determination in drinking water
J Anal Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.S. Miletić, E.T. Pecev-Marinković, Z.M. Grahovac, A.N. Pavlović, S.B. Tošić, I.D.R. Mišić, Kinetic Spectrophotometric Method for 4-nitrophenol Determination in Drinking Water, J Anal Chem. 74 (2019) 521–527. https://doi.org/10.1134/S1061934819060066.
